text: CLEMENT-DE-MONTAGNE (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Allier, cant. of Mayet-de-Montagne, 15 m. SSE of La Palesse, on the r. bank of the Bèbre. Pop. 1,612.
CLE'MENT-DE-LA-PLACE (SAINT), or SAINTJEAN-DES-MARAIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Le Loroux-Beconnais. Pop. 1,296.
CLE'MENT-DE-REIGNAT (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Randans. Pop. 1,135.

CLEMENTE (SAN), a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and partido and 47 m. SW of Cuenca, on the l. bank of the Rus. Pop. 3,120. It contains 6 parish-churches, several convents, a college, an hospital, a custom-house, &c.; and has an extensive trade in cattle and wool.—Also an island in the N Pacific, in N lat. 33°, 48 m. from the coast of Upper California, and 15 m. S of Santa Catalina island. It is about 30 m. in circumf.

CLEMENTE (SANTO), a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Lavoro, district and 11 m. SE of Caserte, in a plain. Pop. 1,250. It possesses 3 CLE 4,340 acres. Pop. 1,351. It contains the hamlet of j churches, and a museum containing a valuable collection of ancient Greek and Latin.
CLEMENTI, a town of Turkey, in the pash. of Scutari. Pop. 1,500.
CLEMENTIN (SAINT), a village of France, in the dep. of the Deux-Sevres, cant. and 8 m. SW of Argenton-le-Château. Pop. 1,200.

CLEMENT'S (St.), a parish of Cornwall, 13 m. SE of Truro. Area 3,520 acres. Pop. 3,436.—Also a parish of Oxfordshire, \(\frac{3}{4}\) m. E of Oxford, from which it is separated by the Cherwell. Area 580 acres. Pop. 1,769. CLEMENT (Sr.) TERRINGTON, a parish of Norfolk. Area 7,380 acres. Pop. 1,675.
CLEMENT'S STRAIT, a channel in the Eastern sea, between Middle island and Long island, forming part of Gaspar strait.
CLEMENTSTOWN, a village in the p. of Kildrumsherdan, co. Cavan. Pop. 229.
CLENCH-WARTON, a parish in Norfolk, 21 m. W of King's Lynn. Area 3,010 acres. Pop. 597.
CLENORE, a parish in co. Cork, 12 m. S of Doneraile. Area 4,289 acres. Pop. 1,348.
CLENT, a parish in Worcestershire, 31 m. SSE of Stourbridge. Area 2,520 acres. Pop. 918.
CLENZE, a town of Hanover, in the prov. of Luneburg, 10 m. NW of Salzwedel.

CLEOBURY-MORTIMER, a parish and markettown in Shropshire, 11 m. E of Ludlow. Area of p. 7,670 acres. Pop. 1,730.—The town is situated on the river Rea. It was the birth-place of Robert Langland, a poet of the 14th cent., who wrote the 'Visions of Pierce Plowman.'
CLEOBURY (NORTH), a parish in Shropshire, 8 m. SW of Bridgenorth. Area 1,710 acres. Pop. 176.
CLE'RAC, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Montguyon, 20 m. S of Jonzac. Pop. 1,434.
CLERCKEN, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of W Flanders, arrond. and 8 m. E by S of Furness. Pop. 2,845.
CLE'RE', a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Langeais, 20 m. NNE of Chinon.

CLE'RES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Rouen.— The cant., comprising 22 com., had a pop. of 13,529 in 1841.—The com. is 10 m. N of Rouen, on a small stream of the same name, an affluent of the Cailly. Pop. 906.

CLE'RIEUX, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Drôme, cant. of Romans, 12 m. from Valence. Pop. 1,678. There are silk and linen manufactories here, and agricultural implements are extensively made.
CLERJUS (LE), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Vosges, cant. of Xertigny, 17 m. S of Epinal. Pop. 2,333. There are iron forges, and distilleries of kirschwasser.
CLERKE. See CLARKE.

CLERKENWELL, a large out-parish of the city of London, 1 m. NNW of St. Paul's. Pop. in 1801, 23,396; in 1831, 47,634; in 1841, 56,756. It is an ill-conditioned district; the very "low London of low London." Two of the great London prisons stand here, namely, the house-of-detention or general receiving prison for the county, and the house-of-correction, or Cold-bath-fields prison. The former contains 300 cells; the latter, which consists of three distinct divisions, can receive 900 prisoners.

CLERKE'S REEF, or RITCHIE'S REEF, a reef off the coast of W Australia, in S lat. 20° 17', long. 0° 26' W of Swan river, or 115° 21' E. It is nearly 3 m. long, and 12 m. wide, with a dry patch in the centre.—Stokes.
CLERK'S ISLAND (SIR GEORGE), a high island in the Arctic ocean, near the mouth of Croker river, and 10 or 12 m. from the shore, in N lat. 69° 25', W long. 118° 30'.-Franklin.
CLERKE'S ISLAND. See St. LAWRENCE.

CLERMONT, a county in the SW of Ohio, U. S., intersected by the East Fork, a branch of the Little Miami. Area 450 sq. m. Pop. 23,106. Its cap. is Batavia.—Also a township in Columbia co., in the state of New York, 13 m. S of Hudson. Pop. 1,231.
CLERMONT, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of Liége, arrond. and 8 m. N by W of Verviers, 2 m. E of Herve. Pop 2,132. Coal is wrought here.

CLERMONT, or CLERMONTEN-BEAUVOISIN, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of L'Oise.—The arrond has an area of 129,410 hectares, and comprises the 8 cant. of Breteuil, C., Crevecœur, Froissy, Liancourt, Maignelay, and Mouy. Pop. in 1841, 89,347; in 1846, 90,817.— The cant., comprising 23 com., had a pop. of 14,446 in 1841.—The com. and town are on the r. bank of the Brèche, 18 m. E of Beauvais. Pop. in 1841, 4,191. It has cotton manufactories; and an important trade in grain and flour. There is a communal college here, and a public library of 12,000 vols. The ancient chateau of the princes of Condé at this place has been converted into a house-of-correction, which had 804 détentes in 1841.

CLERMONT-EN-ARGONNE, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Meuse, arrond. of Verdun.—The cant., comprising 17 com., had a pop. in 1841 of 10,361.—The com., 17 m. WSW of Verdun, near the Aire, had a pop. of 1,446.
CLERMONT-DESSOUS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, cant. of Pont-Sainte-Marie, on the r. bank of the Garonne. Pop. 1,331.

CLERMONT-FERRAND, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme.—The arrond. has an area of 178,680 hectares. Pop. in 1841, 172,238; in 1846, 176,511. It comprises the 11 cantons of Saint- Amand-Tallende, Billom, Bourg-Lastie, C., Saint- Dier, Herment, Pont-du-Chateau, Rochefort, Vertaizon, Veyres, and Vic-le-Comte.—The cant. comprises 16 com., with a pop. in 1841 of 56,229.—The town, an "unprepossessing and gloomy place," but the cap. of the dep., stands in the centre of an amphitheatre formed on the S and W by the chain of the Puys-de-Dôme between Arrier and Bédat, 216 m. S by E of Paris, in N lat. 45° 46′ 46″, long. 0° 44′ 57″ E of Paris; at an alt. of 411 metres, or 1,348 ft. above sealevel. The plain in which it stands is situated nearly in the centre of France, and spreads out for more than 15 leagues in all the beauty and fertility of one vast continuous garden, forming the celebrated La Limagne, or Limagne d'Auvergne; and the huge central Puy-de- Dome [alt. 4,797 ft.] appears close to the town, though at the distance of 4 m. from it. The town contains several spacious streets, market-places, and halls for cloth and grain; and the cathedral is a fine Gothic edifice, built of the dark lava from Volvic, but never finished, and hemmed-in by mean habitations. The Jardin-des-Plantes, and the Bibliothèque, containing 16,000 vols., are finely situated on the declivity of the hill on part of which C. is built. Some of the fountains are very fine, especially that in the Place Delille, so named after the poet who was a native of C. No important manufactures are carried on in C.; but it has tanneries, breweries, and distilleries, and a considerable quantity of coarse woollens and serges, silk stockings, tinted paper, comfitures, conserves, potatostarch, chocolate, and chemical wares, is made here; and the hemp, cheese, cattle, linen, wines, and fruits of the Limagne, are poured into it on market-days, CLE for transit to Paris, Lyons, or Bordeaux. In the faubourg of St. Alyre is a remarkable spring which rapidly deposits a hard calcareous crust on any object placed in its waters, and has formed a natural dyke 250 ft. in length. There is also a hot mineral spring at St. Mart in the vicinity. Provisions are good and plentiful, but fuel is scarce and dear; but water of a good quality is brought from Royat, about 2 m. distant.—C. is the seat of a bishop, a suffragan of Bourges, and of a royal college of the 3d class, a secondary school of medicine, a primary normal school for the dep., and an Academie universitaire. Pop. in 1789, 31,590; in 1841, 31,500.—C. was anciently the cap. of Auvergne. It was several times taken and ravaged during the wars with the English. The celebrated council, in which Pope Urban II. preached the first crusade, was held here in 1095. On the incorporation of the little town of Montferrand, now forming one of its suburbs, with C. in 1633, it took its present compound name. It was the birth-place of Gregory of Tours, of Pascal, of Domat, Desaix, Thomas, Chamfort, and the chevalier d'Assas.—For the geological features of the neighbourhood, see article AUVERGNE.
CLERMONT-GALLERANDE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, cant. and 3 m. NE of La Fleche. Pop. 1,444.

CLERMONT-DE-LODE'VE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Hérault, arrond. of Lodève.—The cant., comprising 15 com., had a pop. of 14,188 in 1841.—The town, 8 m. SE of Lodève, on the Ronnel, had a pop. of 6,226. It is a place of active industry. Woollen cloth is extensively manufactured here; and there are also vinegar, brandy, vitriol, leather, and brick works.

CLERMONT-TONNERRE, or MINERVA ISLAND, an island in the Pacific, in S lat. 18° 33' 42", W long. 136° 01′ 32". It is 10 m. in length, but very narrow; and abounds with cocoa-nut trees. Pop. about 200. Captain Duperrey visited this island in the Coquille, and supposing it to be a new discovery, named it after the then French minister of marine; but Captain Beechey is of opinion that this island had been previously discovered by the Minerva.

CLERVAL, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Doubs, arrond. of Baume-les- Dames.—The cant. comprises 25 com. Pop. in 1841, 9,316.—The town, 6 m. NE of Baume-les-Dames, on the Doubs, had a pop. of 1,097.
CLERVAUX, or CLERFF, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Luxembourg, arrond. and 14 m. N of Diekirch. Pop. in 1835, 1,445.

CLE'RY, or Notre-Dame-de-Cle'ry, a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Loiret, arrond. of Orleans.—The cant., comprising 5 com., had a pop. of 5,870 in 1841.—The town, 10 m. SW of Orleans, on the l. bank of the Loire, had a pop. of 2,510. It contains a fine church, within which Louis XI. was buried.
CLESS, or CLOSS, a town of Austria, in Tyrol, in the Nonsbergthal, on the r. bank of the Noce, 18 m. N by W of Trent.
CLESSE', a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Lugny. Pop. 1,161.
CLET (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, cant. of Pontrieux. Pop. 1,520.
CLETTER (SAINT), a parish in Cornwall, 7 m. E of Camelford. Area 3,540 acres. Pop. 221.
CLETTERWOOD, a township in the p. of Buttington, Montgomeryshire, 8 m. from Montgomery. Pop. 275.

CLEVEDON, a parish and village in Somersetshire, 12 m. W by S of Bristol. Area of p. 2,970 acres. Pop. 1,748.—The v. stands on a cliff on the shore of the British channel, and has considerable repute as a bathing-place. It is within 4 m. of Yatton, on the Bristol and Exeter railway.
CLEVEDON-MILTON, a parish in Somersetshire, 21 m. NNE of Bruton. Area 1,320 acres. Pop. 213.

CLEVELAND, the capital of Cuyahoga co., in the state of Ohio, U.S., on Lake Erie, at the mouth of the Cuyahoga river, and the N termination of the Ohio canal, in N lat. 41° 31′, W long. 81° 46′, 130 m. NW of Pittsburg, 146 m. NE of Columbus, 130 m. by water from Detroit, and 200 m. from Buffalo. Excepting a small portion of it immediately on the Cuyahoga, the town is situated on a gravelly plain, elevated about 80 ft. above the level of the lake, of which it has a very commanding prospect. The streets have the width of 80 ft.; and Main-street, which passes through the middle of the place, is 120 ft. wide. The streets cross at right angles, and there are some expensive and tasteful edifices. The site is dry and healthy, and commands a fine view of the meanderings of the Cuyahoga, and of the steam-boats and shipping leaving or entering the port. Near the centre of the town is a public square of 10 acres, divided into four equal parts by intersecting streets, neatly enclosed, and shaded with trees. The harbour of C. is one of the best on Lake Erie. It is formed by the mouth of the Cuyahoga, and a pier on each side, extending 425 yds. into the lake, and 200 ft. apart.—C. is the great mart of the greatest grain-growing state in the Union; and owes this pre-eminence to the Ohio and Erie canal; though it exports considerably by the way of the Welland canal to Canada. The exports from C. to Canada in 1848 were valued at 142,312 dollars, against 23,969 d. of imports from Canada. It has a ready connection with Pittsburg, through the Pennsylvania and Ohio canal, which extends from the Ohio canal at Akron to Beaver creek, which enters the Ohio below Pittsburg. Wheat, flax, oats, gypsum, anthracite, pig-iron, flour, butter, pork, bacon, cheese, staves, and timber, are among the chief articles of arrival or transit at C. There is a lighthouse on the high bank of the lake; and another at the entrance of the harbour. Ohio, on the opposite side of the Cuyahoga, constitutes virtually one place with C. It has, however, a separate corporation. The pop. of C. in 1825 was 500; in 1834, 4,300; in 1840, 6,071. The municipal authority of C. consists of a mayor, 3 aldermen, and 9 councilmen.—C. derives its name from Moses Cleveland, an agent of the Connecticut Land co., under whose direction the site for the town was surveyed in 1796. It was incorporated as a village in 1814, and as a town in 1836.

CLEVELAND, a county in the SW part of the state of N Carolina, U.S. Area 550 sq. m. The cap. is Shelby.—Also a village in Oswego co., in the state of New York, on the N side of Oneida lake. Pop. 300.—Also the cap. of Bradley co., in the state of Tennessee.—Also a v. in Merriwether co., in the state of Georgia.—Also a v. in Winnebago co., in Illinois.
CLEVELAND BAY, an indentation of the NE coast of Australia, between Cape Cleveland in S lat. 19° 10′ 10′, and E long. 146° 57′ 56″, and Magnetical island.

CLEVELEY, a hamlet in the p. of Enstone, Oxfordshire, 1½ m. SSE of Neat-Enstone. Pop. 198.— Also a township, partly in the parishes of Garstang and Cockerham, 41 m. NNE of Garstang, Lancashire. Area 530 acres. Pop. 124.
CLEVERTON. See LEA and CLEVERTON.

CLEVES, or KLEVE, a circle of Prussia, in the gov. of Dusseldorf, prov. of the Rhine. The present CLI circle is bounded on the N by Holland, on the E by the Rhine which separates it from the modern circle of Rees; on the S by the circle of Geldern; and on the W by Holland. The former regency of C. embraced both sides of the Rhine; and extended considerably further to the S, comprising the circles of C., Duislaken, Geldern, Kempen, Rees, and Rheinberg. Area of the circle 185 sq. m. The pop. in 1831 was 42,194, of whom 37,603 were Roman Catholics, and 4,084 Protestants.—Its cap., of the same name, is situated on two or three small elevations in a fertile plain on the Kermisdal, and within 2 m. of the Rhine, with which it communicates by a canal. It is walled, and generally well-built; and has a pop. of 7,700. It has considerable manufactures, particularly of woollens, and cotton and silk goods,-Prussia acquired C. as early as 1609. The duchy was ceded to France in December 1805, and in 1806 Napoleon erected it into a grand duchy, which he bestowed upon Murat. In 1815 Prussia resumed possession of the duchy.
CLEVES, a village of Miami township, Hamilton co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 128 m. SW of Columbus, on the Big Miami river, half-a-mile from its junction with the Ohio. Pop. in 1840, 150.

CLEW BAY, a capacious and highly picturesque bay, on the W coast of co. Mayo. It enters between Achilbeg on the N, and Buinaha-point on the S; and extends 141 m. eastward, with a breadth somewhat uniform, and averaging about 51 m. The outline of all its head, and of about 6 m. and 31 m. respectively down its N and S sides, presents a diversified series of little bays and peninsulæ; and the belt of from 6 to 3 m. across the head is a crowded archipelago of islets, which vie with one another in beauty, and are variously estimated in number at nearly 100, at 170, at upwards of 300, and at nearly 400! The shores W of the archipelago are bold, rocky, occasionally mountainous, and prevailingly unindented; they consist, on the N, of the grand sandstone heights of Coraan Achill, fringed in some spots with low limestone reefs; and, on the S, of shivery quartz rock, serpentine, and granite, towering aloft in cliff and eminence, and immediately presided over by the grand-monarch mountain of the W, the magnificent Croagh-Patrick. The island of Clare [which see] occupies one-third of the entrance of the bay, and largely contributes to both the beauty of its scenery and the safety of its harbourage and navigation. The profusion of isles in the archipelago are composed of a deep loamy soil upon a basis of limestone; and, excepting unimportant islets, are all fertile and well-cultivated. Nearly the whole bay is clear of danger to navigation; its labyrinth of creeks and sounds, at the upper end, presents a multitude of safe harbours and roadsteads; its creeks of Oldhead, Mynish, Doobeg, and some others, are in use as fishery-stations; and its chief expansions at the N and S corners of its head form the bays or harbours of Newport and Westport: which see. Upwards of 20,000 persons on its shores and islands are supposed to be wholly or partly employed and maintained by its fisheries.
CLEWER, a parish of Berks, 1 m. W of Windsors on the S bank of the Thames. Area 1,490 acres- Pop. 3,975.
CLEWILSEY, a township in the p. of Llanvair- Waterdine, Salop. Pop. 206.
CLEY-NEXT-THE-SEA, a parish, market-town, and sea-port of Norfolk, 25 m. NNW of Norwich, 8 m. W of Wells, and 41 m. NNW of Holt on the Glaven, about a mile from the beach.
CLIBURN, a parish of Westmoreland, 7 m. NW of Appleby, on the Lyvennet. Area 1,360 acres. Pop. 251.

CLICHY-LA-GARENNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine, cant. of Neuilly, in a fine plain, near the r. bank of the Seine, 5 m. NW of Paris, and on the railroad from the cap. to St. Germain. Pop. 4,189. It contains extensive chemical works, manufactories of white-lead, sugar of lead, &c. In the environs are numerous handsome villas and a castle, in which a council was held in 636 and in 1351.
CLIDDESDEN, a parish of Southampton, 13 m. S of Basingstoke. Area 2,150 acres. Pop. 306.

CLIFDEN, a post and thriving seaport town in the p. of Omay, co. Galway, Connaught, finely situated on an offshoot of Ardbear bay, at the foot of the lofty mountain-range named the Twelve Pins of Binnabola, 2 m. SSE of Streamstown, 26 m. NW of Oughterard, and 40 m. WNW of Galway. Pop. in 1831, 1,257; in 1841, 1,509.—Also a village in the p. of Blanchvilleskill, co. Kilkenny, Leinster.
CLIFF, or CLIVE, a township in the p. of Middlewich, Cheshire. Area 1,130 acres. Pop. 117.
CLIFF-CUM-LUND, a township in the p. of Hemingborough, E. R. of Yorkshire, 31 m. E of Selby, and 28 m. from Hull, and intersected by the Leeds and Hull railway. Area 2,280 acres. Pop. 540.
CLIFF (NORTH), a township in the p. of Sancton, E. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. S of Market-Weighton, and near the Hull and Selby railway. Area 1,480 acres. Pop. 74.
CLIFF (South), a township in the p. of North Cave, E. R. of Yorkshire, 31 m. S of Market-Weighton. Area 1,700 acres. Pop. 136.
CLIFFE, a township in the p. of Manfield, N. R. of Yorkshire, 51 m. NW of Darlington. Area 970 acres. Pop. 54.
CLIFFE (EAST), an extra-parochial district of Kent. Pop. 205.
CLIFFE-AT-HOO, a parish of Kent, 6 m. N of Rochester, and S of the Thames. Area 5,400 acres. Pop. 842.
CLIFFE-PYPARD, or CLEEVE PEPPER, a parish of Wilts, 4 m. S of Wootton-Basset. Area 5,050 acres. Pop. 933.
CLIFFE (West), a parish of Kent, 24 m. N of Dover. Area 1,090 acres. Pop. 116.
CLIFFONEY, a village in the p. of Ahamlish, co. and 10 m. N of Sligo, Connaught, near the sea, 11 m. N of Mullaghmore, on the road from Sligo to Ballyshannon.
CLIFFORD, a parish of Herefordshire, 8 m. SSW of Kington, and SE of the Wye. Area 6,920 acres. Pop. 892.
CLIFFORD-CUM-BOSTON, a township in the p. of Braham, W. R. of Yorkshire, 3 m. SSE of Wetherby, S of the Warfe. Area 1,500 acres. Pop. 1,566.
CLIFFORD-CHAMBERS, a parish of Gloucestershire, 2 m. S of Stratford-on-Avon. Area 1,530 acres. Pop. 309.

CLIFTON, a parish and village in the co. of Gloucester, about 1 m. W of Bristol, on the N side of the river Avon. Area of p. 910 acres. Houses in 1831, 1,605; in 1841, 1,907. Pop. in 1801, 4,457; in 1831, 12,032; in 1841, 14,177. The village or rather town of C. is naturally divided into two parts,— Upper and Lower C.,—the difference of elevation betwixt them being about 220 ft. Upper C. lies on the brow of a hill in a convex or semicircular form, abutting on the SW over the river Avon, and extending to the SE, or towards Bristol about 1 m. Immediately in front lies Lower C. and the floating-harbour of Bristol. C. faces to the S and SW; and is sheltered from the N and NW winds by the C. and Durdham downs. Upper C., situated on the summit of a precipitous cliff, commands charming views of the surrounding country, including the highly cultivated lands of Somersetshire and the W portion of the city of Bristol. The Avon beneath winds between cliffs which shoot precipitously up to a height so nearly equal, and with such exact correspondence of the strata, that hardly a doubt can be entertained that it was formed by some violent natural convulsion. In Lower C. also the scenery is of an imposing character, but the valley is too narrow, and the houses and other objects on the heights too crowded, and devoid of fore-ground to give effect to the natural characteristics. Many of the acclivities have been laid out and fitted up as parades, terraces, crescents, &c. A design has been formed of uniting the preci- EXPLOSE AND PROCEEDING AND CONTRACTOR OF THE PROCESS OF THE PROCESS OF THE PROCESS OF THE PROCESS OF THE PROCESS OF THE PROCESS OF THE PROCESS OF THE PROCESS OF THE PROCESS OF THE PROCESS OF THE PROCESS OF THE PROCESS OF T CLI pitous banks of the Avon by a bridge, and this undertaking was begun, and £40,231 expended upon it up to February 1843, but it is calculated that it will require £75,000, and little further progress has been made in it. The roadway of this bridge will be 850 ft. in length, and 220 ft. above high water mark.- Assemblies and concerts, with all the other amusements necessary for a gay watering-place, are kept up at C. by subscription; and races are held in the month of April. For the purposes of parliamentary representation and municipal jurisdiction, the whole of this p. has been included within the bounds of the city of Bristol, and Clifton made, in every respect, part of the city and co. of Bristol. The assessed property of the p. in 1815 was returned at £4,535; in 1842-3 it was returned to the property tax at £92,432, whereof £89,101 was on houses. The amount of poor rates in 1838 was 6,426; in 1842, £7,744. The C. poor-law union comprehends 12 parishes, embracing an area of 27,808 acres; with a pop. returned in 1831 at 51,345; in 1841 at 66,233. The average annual expenditure on the poor of this district during the three years preceding the formation of the union was £17,863. Expenditure in 1838, £15,352; in 1843, £16,744; in 1846, £15,537. A small neat chapel, built and endowed by subscription near King's parade, was consecrated in 1841. There are a private Episcopal chapel, one in Lady Huntingdon's connexion, and a floating chapel for seamen here; besides an Independent chapel, and a Roman Catholic church of Greek architecture.—Clifton possesses a remarkably mild and balmy atmosphere, from which it has been styled the Montpelier of England. The average annual number of deaths within the parish for three years 1840-2, was 2.6 per cent., or 1 in 38.55; within the poor-law union it was 2.5 per cent., or 1 in 40.50. This forms a striking contrast to the mortality of Bristol. There is, however, a great difference of mortality betwixt the upper and lower divisions of the p.; that of the former being 1.6 per cent., or 1 in 61.6; while that of the latter was 3.4 per cent., or 1 in 28.5. C. owes much of its importance to its hot medicinal wells, which have been in general repute in cases of debility, consumption, scorbutic diseases, dysentery, &c., for two hundred years. The mineral contents of these celebrated waters are similar to those of Matlock. The principal components are an uncommon quantity of carbonic acid gas and fixed air, and a portion of magnesia in various combinations with the muriatic, sulphuric, and carbonic acids. The temp. of the water at the pump is between 72° and 73° Fahrenheit. It is lighter than common water, clear, pure, soft, and pleasant. The spring rises near the bottom of the cliff, 10 ft. below watermark, and gushes so plenteously from an aperture in the solid rock, as to discharge 60 gallons in a minute.

CLIFTON, a parish of Bedfordshire, 1½ m. E of Shefford, and W of the Ivel. Area 1,420 acres. Pop. 865.—Also a township in the p. of Eccles, Lancashire, 5 m. NW of Manchester, on the Bury and Bolton canal. Area 820 acres. Pop. 1,360.—Also a township in the p. of Deddington, Oxfordshire. Area 670 acres. Pop. 277.—Also a parish of Westmoreland, 3 m. SSE of Penrith, on the post-road. Area 1,520 acres. Pop. 288.—Also a township partly in the parishes of St. Michael-le-Belfry and St. Olave Marygate, N. R. of Yorkshire, 14 m. NW of York, on the Ouse. Pop. 861.-Also a hamlet in the p. of Conisbrough, W. R. of Yorkshire. Pop. 106.—Also a village in Strathfillan, Perthshire. Pop. 159.
CLIFTON, a village of Miami township, Green co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 52 m. WSW of Columbus, at the falls of the Little Miami. Pop. in 1840, 350.
CLIFTON, or ROCKSAVAGE, a township in the p. of Runcorn, Cheshire. Area 640 acres. Pop. 34.
CLIFTON-CAMPVILLE, a parish and township partly in Derbyshire and Staffordshire, 5 m. NE of Tamworth, and S of the Meese. Area 5,470 acres. Pop. 921. Pop. of township 341.
CLIFTON-UPON-DUNSMORE, a parish of Warwickshire, 21 m. ENE of Rugby, S of the Avon, and on the Oxford canal. Area 5,160 acres. Pop. 364.
CLIFTON (GREAT), a chapelry in the p. and 25 m. E of Workington, Cumberland, on the S bank of the Derwent. Pop. 378.
CLIFTON-WITH-GLAPTON, a parish of Nottinghamshire, 4 m. SSW of Nottingham, on the E bank of the Trent. Area 1,980 acres. Pop. 419.
CLIFTON-HAMPDEN, a parish of Oxfordshire, 31 m. S of Abingdon, bounded on the S by the Thames. Area 1,030 acres. Pop. 297.
CLIFTON-CUM-HARTSHEAD, a chapelry in the p. of Dewsbury, W. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. NE of Huddersfield. Area 2,820 acres. Pop. 2,675.
CLIFTON (LITTLE), a township in the p. and 33 m. E of Workington, on a branch of the Derwent. Pop. 281.
CLIFTON-MAYBANK, a parish of Dorset, 4½ m. WSW of Sherborne. Area 770 acres. Pop. 70.
CLIFTON (NORTH), a parish of Nottinghamshire, 5 m. ENE of Tuxford. Area 5,050 acres. Pop. 1,056.
CLIFTON-WITH-NORWOOD, a township in the p. of Fewston, W. R. of Yorkshire, 5 m. N of Otley. Area 3,510 acres. Pop. 387.

CLIFTON PARK, a township of Saratoga co., in the state of New York, U. S., 17 m. NW of Albany, bordered on the S by the Mohawk. Its surface is undulating, and its soil, consisting of clay and sandy Ioam, generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 2,719.
CLIFTON (POINT), a headland on the coast of the Arctic ocean, in N lat. 69° 13', W long. 118° 22'. The coast here consists of limestone in horizontal layers.
CLIFTON-REYNES, a parish of Bucks, about a mile E of Olney, bounded on the N and W by the Ouse. Area 1,120 acres. Pop. 213.
CLIFTON-WITH-SALWICK, a township in the p. and 31 m. ESE of Kirkham, Lancashire, at the mouth of the Ribble. Area 3,010 acres. Pop. 538.
CLIFTON (South), a township in the p. of North Clifton, Nottinghamshire, 5 m. E of Tuxford. Pop. 332.
CLIFTON-UPON-TEAME, a parish of Worcestershire, 10 m. WNW of Worcester. Area 3,310 acres. Pop. 512.
CLIFTON-UPON-URE, a township in the p. of Thornton-Watlas, N. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SW of Bedale. Area 720 acres. Pop. 39.
CLIFTY, a township of Bartholomew co., in the state of Indiana, U. S. Pop. in 1840, 1,082.

CLIMAX (MONT), or SIVRI-DAGH, a mountainridge in Asiatic Turkey, on the W coast of the gulf of Adalia, crossed by the parallel of 36° 45' N. Its peaks are rugged and picturesque; and the whole is a rugged range of pine-clad precipitous rocks.
CLIMAX PRAIRIE, a township of Kalamazoo co., in the state of Michigan, U. S. Pop. in 1840, 301.
CLIMPING, a parish of Sussex, 4 m. S of Arundel, W of the Arun, and near the Arundel and Portsmouth canal. Area 2,060 acres. Pop. 279.

CLINCH, a river of the United States, which takes its rise in the state of Virginia; passes into that of Tennessee; and after a total course of about 200 m. unites with the Holston river at Kingston, and forms the Tennessee. It is navigable for small boats the greater part of its course.-Also a town- CLI ship of Van Buren co., in the state of Michigan. Pop. in 1840, 250.

CLINCH MOUNTAINS, an extensive range of mountains in the United States, a branch of the Alleghany chain, extending between the 36th and 37th parallels, in the states of Virginia and Tennessee, and forming the line of separation between the beds of the Clinch and Holston rivers.
CLINCHAMPS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Saint-Sever, 4 m. WNW of Vire. Pop. 1,654.
CLINOVA. See KLINOVA.
CLINT, a township in the p. and 11 m. WSW of Ripley, W. R. of Yorkshire, N of the Nidd. Area 1,750 acres. Pop. 393.
CLINT, a mountain of Berwickshire, in the range of the Lammermuirs, 18 m. SE of Edinburgh, which rises to the height of 1,544 ft. above sea-level.

CLINTON, a county in the state of New York, U. S., comprising an area of 932 sq. m., bounded by Lower Canada on the N, and by Lake Champlain on the E, and watered by Saranac, Chazy, and Great and Little Ausable rivers, which afford extensive water-power. It is hilly in the S; but its northern parts are level and well-cultivated. Pop. in 1840, 28,157. Its cap. is Plattsburg. There are extensive iron-mines in this co.-Also a co. in the state of Pennsylvania, comprising an area of 840 sq. m., intersected by the Susquehanna. It is partly mountainous, but on the banks of the streams extremely fertile. Pop. 8,323. Its cap. is Lock Haven.—Also a county in the state of Kentucky, comprising an area of 200 sq. m., drained by several small branches of Cumberland river. It possesses a diversified surface, but is generally fertile. Pop. 3,863. Its cap. is Albany.-Also a co. in the state of Michigan, containing an area of 400 sq. m., watered by branches of Little Miami river, which afford good water-power. Its surface is undulating, and its soil fertile. Pop. in 1830, 11,292; in 1840, 15,719. The cap. is Wilmington.—Also a co. in the state of Michigan, comprising a level surface of 576 sq. m., drained by Maple and Looking-glass rivers. It is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 1,614. The cap. is De Witt.—Also a co. in the state of Indiana, containing an area of 432 sq. m., drained by branches of Wildcat and Sugar creeks. With the exception of the Twelve-Mile prairie, the soil is fertile, and covered with good timber. Pop. in 1830, 1,423; in 1840, 7,508. The cap. is Frankfort.—Also a co. in the state of Illinois, comprising an area of 480 sq. m., intersected by Kaskaskia river. Its surface is undulating and its soil fertile. Pop. in 1830, 2,330; in 1840, 3,718. The cap. is Carlisle.— Also a co. in the state of Missouri, containing an area of 425 sq. m., drained by branches of Little river. Its surface is level, and two-thirds of it prairie, and its soil very fertile. Pop. in 1840, 2,724. The cap. is Plattsburg.—Also a co. of the Iowa territory, drained by Wabesipinica river and its branches, Prairie and Deep creeks, and other branches of Macoquitais river. Pop. 821. The cap. is Camanche. -Also a township of Kennebec co., in the state of Maine, 25 m. NNE of Augusta, bordered on the W by the Kennebec, and intersected by Sebasticook river, which affords extensive water-power, and on which the village is situated. The soil is generally fertile. Pop. 2,818.—Also a township of Middlesex co., in the state of Connecticut, 51 m. SE of Hartford. Pop. 1,239.—Also a township of Dutchess co., in the state of New York, 10 m. N of Ploughkeepsie. The surface is hilly; but the soil, consisting of gravelly loam, is well-cultivated. Pop. 1,830-Also a village of Kirkland township, Oneida co., in the state of New York, pleasantly situated on both sides of Oriskany creek, 99 m. WNW of Albany. Pop. about 800.—Also a village of Hunterdown co., in the state of New Jersey, on the S branch of Raritan river, and partly in Lebanon, Bethlehem, and Kingwood townships.—Also a township of Essex co., in the state of New Jersey. Pop. 1,976. Also a township of Wayne co., in the state of Pennsylvania. Pop. 516.—Also a township of Lycoming co., in the same state, 6 m. SE of Williamsport, in a bend of the W branch of the Susquehanna. Pop. 1,193.— Also a village of Tecumseh township, Lenawee co., in the state of Michigan, 52 m. SW of Detroit, on the E bank of the N branch of the Raisin river, which affords good water-power. Pop. about 600.- Also a township of Macomb co., in the same state, bordering on Lake St. Clair, and watered by Red river. It is generally fertile. Pop. 1,115.—Also a township of Vermillion co., in the state of Indiana, 82 m. W of Indianapolis. Pop. 1,296. The village is on the W bank of Wabash river.—Also a township of Putnam co., in the same state. Pop. 1,218.— Also a village in Hickman co., in the state of Kentucky, 298 m. WSW of Frankfort, on the N side of Bayou Desha.—Also a village in Sampson co., in the state of North Carolina, 94 m. SSE of Raleigh, on a branch of Black river.—Also a village in Jones co., in the state of Georgia, 26 m. W of Milledgeville.— Also a village of Hind's co., in the state of Mississippi, 10 m. NW of Jackson. Pop. 400. It contains the Mississippi college.—Also a village in Van Buren co., in the state of Arkansas, 90 m. N of Little Rock, on the S side of Little Red river.—Also a village in Anderson co., in the state of Tennessee, 175 m. E of Nashville, on the N bank of Clinch river.—Also a village in Dewitt co., in the state of Illinois, 64 m. ENE of Springfield, on Salt creek, a branch of Sangamon river.—Also a village in Rives co., in the state of Missouri, near the N bank of Grand river .- Also a township—formerly called Pike—of Summit co., in the state of Ohio, 118 m. NW of Columbus, watered by Indian creek, a branch of Tuscarawas river.—Also a township of Franklin co., in the same state. Pop. 966.—Also a township of Lucas co., in the same state. Pop. 353.—Also a township of Jackson co., in the same state. Pop. 824.—Also a township of Seneca co., in the same state. Pop. 2,195 .- Also a township of Shelby co., in the same state. Pop. 783.—Also a township of Franklin co., in the same state. Pop. 966.—Also a township of Wayne co., in the same state. Pop. 873.—Also a township of Knox co., in the same state. Pop. 920.—Also a township in the Niagara district, in Upper Canada, bounded on the N by Lake Ontario. Pop. in 1841, 2,122.

CLINTON-COLDEN LAKE, a fine sheet of water in Arctic America, intersected centrally by the parallel of 64° 10' N, and the meridian of 107° 30' W. It is upwards of 60 m. in length from E to W, and is connected on the S with Artillery lake, and on the W with Lake Aylmer. It was discovered by Captain Back in 1833.

CLINTONVILLE, a village of Sable township, Clinton co., in the state of New York, U. S., 153 m. N of Albany, on the N side of Ausable river, 10 m. W of Port-kent. It contains iron of the best quality. Pop. 750.—Also a village of Greenbriar co., in the state of Virginia, 224 m. NW of Richmond.

CLION, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente-Inferieure, cant. of St. Genis. Pop. 1,085. -Also a commune in the dep. of the Indre, cant. of Chatillon - sur - Indre, 28 m. NW of Châteauroux. Pop. 1,482.
CLION (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Pornic, 13 m. S of Paimbouf. Pop. 2,048. It contains mineral springs.

CLION-USCLAT, a commune of France, in the 117.—Also a chapelry in the p. of St. Mary, in CLI dep. of the Drome, cant. of Loriol, 19 m. from Valence. Pop. 775. It contains manufactories of pottery, pipes, &c.
CLIPPESBY, a parish in Norfolk, 3 m. NE of Acle. Area 1,300 acres. Pop. 123.
CLIPSHAM, a parish in Rutlandshire, 9 m. NNW of Stamford. Area 1,570 acres. Pop. 206.

CLIPSTON, a parish in Northamptonshire, 4 m. SSW of Market, Harborough. Area 2,800 acres. Pop. 859.—Also a township in the p. of Edwinston, in the same co. Pop. 286.—Also a township in the p. of Plumptre, in the same co. Pop. 86.

CLISHEIM, a mountain in the N division of the isle of Harris, the loftiest in the Outer Hebrides. Dr. Macculloch calls it Clisseval, and estimates its height at 2,700 ft., which is certainly too low, if his estimate of the alt. of Langa, in its neighbourhood, at 2,407 ft., be correct; for C. is apparently 800 ft. higher. CLISSA [ILLYRIC, Klis], a strong fortress of Dalmatia, 21 m. ENE of Salona, on a rocky height between Monte-Caprario and Monte-Mossor, commanding the approach to Salona from the NE. It was known to the Romans by the name of Andetrium or Anderium.

CLISSON, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, arrond. of Nantes .- The cant., comprising 7 com., had a pop. of 11,409 in 1841.—The town is in a deep valley, on the Sèvre- Nantaise, at the embouchure of the Moine. Pop. 2,759. It was nearly destroyed in the Vendean war. The road to Poitiers is carried over the valley in which C. stands by a lofty viaduct of 12 arches. On the opposite side of the river are the noble remains of the old castle of C.
CLIST-HONISTON, a parish in Devonshire, 4 m. E of Exeter. Area 1,410 acres. Pop. 467.
CLIST-HYDEN, a parish in Devonshire, 3 m. SSE of Collumpton. Area 3,000 acres. Pop. 325.
CLIST-SAINT-GEORGE, a parish in Devonshire, 2 m. NE of Topsham. Area 860 acres. Pop. 370.
CLIST-SAINT-LAWRENCE, a parish in Devonshire, 5 m. S of Collumpton. Area 1,180 acres. Pop. 168.
CLIST-SAINT-MARY, a parish in Devonshire, 11 m. NE of Topsham. Area 580 acres. Pop. 197.

CLITHEROE, a chapelry and borough in the p. of Whalley, in Lancashire, 30 m. N of Manchester, and 24 m. SE of Lancaster, on the E bank of the Ribble. Area of p. 2,410 acres. Pop. 6,765. There are extensive cotton manufactories, print-works, and lime-works here.—The parl borough comprises the chapelries of C. and Downham, and the townships of Whalley, Wiswall, Pendleton, and Henshorn. Pop. in 1841, 11,324. Electors in 1847, 497. Two railways have been projected to connect C. with the great lines of the co. One, 39 m. in length, it was proposed should commence at Accrington on the East Lancashire railway, and terminate at Settle on the Lancaster and Skipton branch of the North Western railway. The other, 10 m. in length, was to run from C. to Accrington, by Wiswall, Whalley, Enfield, and Church. CLITUNNO, — the ancient Clitumnus, — a small stream in the Papal states, in the deleg. of Spoletoe-Rieti. It rises near Le Vene, springing from the earth at once with a full stream [Simond], and joins the Maroggia after a course of 6 m. through a fine pastoral district, in which still, as in Virgil's time, are grazed herds of milk-white beeves. Within 100 yards of its source, on the acclivity of a bank overlooking and fronting the stream, is an ancient temple of Corinthian order.
CLIVE, a township in the p. and 2 m. SW of Middlewich, in Cheshire. Area 1,130 acres. Pop. Shropshire, 3 m. SSE of Wem. Area 1,370 acres. Pop. 273.
CLIVIGER, a chapelry in the p. of Whalley, in Lancashire, 31 m. SE of Burnley. Area 6,160 acres. Pop. 1,395.
CLIXBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 6 m. E by S of Glandford-Bridge. Area 2,020 acres. Pop. 45.
CLOACHNABEIN, or CLOCHNABANE, a mountain in the p. of Strachan, in Kincardineshire. Alt. 2,370 ft. It is one of the Grampian range, and forms a landmark to ships approaching Aberdeen.

CLOATES (POINT), a headland on the W coast of Australia, in S lat. 22° 35', on a part of the coast to the S of Vlaming-head, which appears to have been taken for an island by the navigators who preceded Captain King in the exploration of this coast.
CLOCAENOG, a parish in Denbighshire, 3 m. SW of Ruthin. Pop. 451.

CLOCH, or CLOUGH POINT, a point of land on the S shore of the frith of Clyde, in the co. of Renfrew, about 5 m. below the port of Greenock, in N lat. 55° 56′ 36″, W long. 4° 52′ 40″. There is a light-house here. The light exhibited is white and stationary; elevated 76 ft. above high water; and seen at the distance of 12 m. in clear weather.

CLODDAGH, a small stream of Queen's and King's cos., in Ireland, rising in the Slievebloom mountains, and falling into the Brosna, 1 m. SW of Ballycumber, after a N and W course of 15 m.- Also a rivulet in co. Waterford, an affluent of the Suir.
CLODOCK, a parish in Herefordshire, 15 m. SW of Hereford. Area 18,250 acres. Pop. 1,762.
CLOFORD, a parish in Somersetshire, 4 m. SW of Frome. Area 2,080 acres. Pop. 253.
CLOGHAN, a village in the p. of Gallen, King's co., 2 m. E of Shannon harbour. Pop. 664.
CLOGHANE, or CLAHANE, a parish and village 13 m. W by S of Tralee, co. Kerry. Area of p. 17,572 acres. Pop. of p. 2,994; of v. 206.
CLOGHEEN, a market-town in the p. of Shanrahan, co. Tipperary, 11 m. SW by W of Clonmel. Pop. 2,049. There are cavalry barracks here.

CLOGHER, a parish in co. Tyrone, comprising an area of 49,761 acres, with a pop. of 17,813. The first 9 m. of the basin of the Blackwater is nearly identical with the surface of this p. The vale of the stream is the principal low ground. The hills on the S and W are of considerable height, especially Tattymale and Slievebeagh, which have altitudes above sea-level respectively of 1,031 ft. and 1,254 ft. The p. contains the towns of C., Augher, and Five-mile- Town.

CLOGHER, an ancient episcopal city and corporate town, but at present merely a village in the above p., is situated on the Blackwater, 7 m. SE by S of Fintona, and 76 m. NNW of Dublin. The cathedral is a cruciform structure, of plain exterior, but well fitted up in the interior. The Episcopal palace, a large modern structure, stands in the midst of a fine park, 500 acres in area. C. was incorporated by letters-patent of 5th Charles I., and had conferred on it 700 acres of land as borough property; but, partially subjected to the bishops of C. in its constitution, it speedily, with all its property and rights, became wholly theirs in practical working. Pop. 702.—The diocese of C. was complete, uniform, and separate, before the passing of the Church temporalities act; but is now united to the diocese of Armagh. Its length from NW to SE is 60 Irish or 76 statute m.; its breadth 20 Irish or 25 statute m.; and its area 819,574 acres. Pop. in 1831, 388,608. The gross episcopal revenue of the see, on an average of 3 years ending in 1831, was £10,371 Os. 2d.; and the net revenue £8,668 11s. 6d. The number of parishes, considering perpetual curacies as such, is 46; benefices 45,— CLO only two of the parishes being united; and the number of chapelries not having separate incumbents is 13. The gross revenue of the benefices is £19,695 16s. <math>7\frac{1}{2}</math>d. from tithe composition, £8,303 3s. 3d. from glebes, and £89 from other sources,—in all £28,087 19s. <math>10\frac{1}{2}</math>d.; and the net revenue £24,334 9s. <math>11\frac{3}{4}</math>d. The number of places of worship is 66 churches and chapels of the Establishment, 32 Presbyterian meetinghouses, 30 meeting-houses belonging to other Protestant dissenting bodies, and 81 Roman Catholic chapels. In 1834 the pop. consisted of 104,359 members of the Established church, 34,623 Presbyterians, 26 other Protestant dissenters, and 260,241 Roman Catholics.—The Roman Catholic diocese of C. is separate, and without annexation. It is distributed into 37 parishes; its bishop resides at Carrickmacross; its diocesan college is at Monaghan; and the number of its parochial and its coadjutor clergy is respectively 35 and 52.
CLOGHER, a parish 6 m. SW of Thurles, co. Tipperary. Area 8,119 acres. Pop. 2,643.
CLOGHER, a fishing-village in co. Mayo, adjacent to Kilcummin-head, about 4 m. N of Killala. It is remarkable as the place at which the French expedition under Humbert landed in 1798.

CLOGHER, or KILCLOGHER, a parish containing a village of the same name, in co. Louth, 5 m. NE of Drogheda. Area 1,861 acres. Pop. 1,371. Clogherhead, on the coast, is situated in N lat. 53° 47', W long. 6° 12'. It is the most easterly ground in the co., and forms a striking object as seen from the flat sandy beach, though not more than 181 ft. above sealevel. The v. stands at a little cove on the N side of the headland. Pop. 841.
CLOGHERBRIEN, a parish 23 m. WNW of Tralee, co. Kerry, occupying the central part of the peninsula between the bays of Ballyheigue and Tralee. Area 3,410 acres. Pop. 1,444.
CLOGHEREEN, a village in the p. of Killarney, co. Kerry, situated at the base of Mangerton hill, and about midway between the upper lake and the town of Killarney. Pop. in 1831, 145.

CLOGHERNEY, or CLOUGHERNEY, a parish 43 m. SE of Omagh, co. Tyrone. It consists of a main body and a detached district; the latter, 21 m. to the NE, comprising an area of 2,368 acres; the former, containing the villages of Beragh and Seskinore, with an area of 15,423 acres. Pop. of the whole, 7,553.

CLOGHGRENAN, or CLOUGHRENAN, a range of hills constituting the E abutments of the Comer mountains, co. of Carlow. They form the W screen of the rich valley of the Barrow, whence, rising gradually and gracefully, they carry cultivation to their very summit. The ancient castle of C. is situated on the r. bank of the Barrow, 21 m. below Carlow. It was built in the 15th cent., by the earls of Ormonde, to defend a pass between the Barrow and the forest of Grenan.
CLOGHJORDAN, a village in co. Tipperary, 4 m. WNW of Dunkerrin. Pop. 1,164.
CLOGHNAKILTY. See CLONAKILTY.

CLOGHPRIOR, a parish in co. Tipperary, 4 m. SW by S of Borris-o'-Kane. Area 3,724 acres. Pop. 1,306. Lough Ourna lies on the SE boundary; and Lough Clara, W of the centre of the interior, has a surface elevation of 232 ft. above sea-level.
CLOGHRAN, or CLOGHRAN-SWORDS, a parish in co. Dublin, 11 m. S of Swords. Area 1,558 acres. Pop. 549.
CLOHAMON, a village in the p. of Kilrush, co. Wexford, on the river Barrow, 2 m. below Newtownbarry. Pop. 309. Here is a small cotton factory.
CLOHAR-CARNOET, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Finistere, cant. and 4 m. SSW of Quimperlé. Pop. 2,795.
